title: State Container
description: State Container orchestrates initial, loading, empty, loaded, and error branches around asynchronous data. It can render default shared states or custom projected templates for each branch.
intro: Use `ui-state-container` when a view needs a consistent state machine around async data. It keeps `loading`, `empty`, `error`, and `content` rendering in one place, which is usually cleaner than scattering conditionals across the page.
importCode: import { StateContainerComponent, initialState, loadingState, loadedState, errorState, type State } from 'ui';

featureSections.title: Initial, loading, empty, loaded, and error
featureSections.description: The core value of the component is that all major async states are handled in one place with one data contract.

featureSections.title: Empty-on-initial behavior
featureSections.description: Some first-run experiences should show an actionable empty state immediately, while others should stay blank until data actually resolves.

featureSections.title: Overlay loading on existing content
featureSections.description: Overlay loading is useful when data already exists but is being refreshed. It preserves context while still communicating progress.

featureSections.title: Custom loading, empty, and data templates
featureSections.description: You can replace the shared defaults with projected templates when a view needs richer or more specific state rendering.

featureSections.title: Embedded list or panel layout
featureSections.description: In practice, state containers usually sit inside dashboards, feeds, and result panels rather than in isolation.

featureSections.title: Data template without wrapper conditionals
featureSections.description: Projected data templates let you keep the loaded branch close to the state logic without manually branching in the surrounding component.

accessibility.title: State clarity
accessibility.body: The visible branch should make it clear whether the view is loading, empty, failed, or ready. Titles and descriptions passed to the built-in states should be specific enough to explain what is happening.

accessibility.title: Actionable recovery
accessibility.body:
When the container renders empty or error actions, those actions should lead to meaningful next steps.

| Branch | Guidance |
| --- | --- |
| Loading | Explain what is being loaded when the wait is noticeable. |
| Empty | Offer a next step only when one is genuinely available. |
| Error | Prefer direct recovery such as retrying or navigating to help. |

accessibility.title: Custom templates
accessibility.body: If you override loading, empty, error, or data templates, preserve the same clarity and keyboard accessibility that the built-in state components provide.

apiSections.title: StateContainerComponent - ui-state-container
apiSections.lead: Standalone orchestration component for async view states. It accepts a shared `State<T>` model and renders loading, error, empty, initial, or data content using built-in state components or projected templates.

blocks.title: Inputs
rows: Shared size passed to built-in state components.
Treats the initial branch as empty when no explicit initial template is provided.
Forces or suppresses empty-state rendering regardless of inferred data emptiness.
Disables internal scrolling so the parent layout owns scroll behavior.

blocks.title: Inputs
rows: Text passed to the built-in loading branch.
Recovery actions for the built-in error branch.
Actions for the built-in empty branch.

paragraphs: The component is most useful when your view already models async data as a shared `State<T>` object.
Use custom templates only when the built-in empty, loading, or error states are not expressive enough. Otherwise keep the state machine simple and consistent.
blocks.title: Notes
